tech.headline: New cooling panel uses PV and waste cooking oil
tech.summary: Researchers have designed an innovative home cooling panel system. The design incorporates PV power generation, thermoelectric cooling, and latent heat storage using waste cooking oil. Through optimized modeling, results suggest that a single panel can deliver approximately 6–15 kWh/day of cooling, providing a potential new method for residential temperature management using renewable energy.

tech.headline: Chile sets bidding terms for first energy auction including storage
tech.summary: Chile’s CNE has released preliminary terms for the 2026/01 power supply tender. This technology-neutral process favors renewables by excluding high-emission fuels and allowing storage-backed bids. Utilizing 15-year contracts and time-of-day demand blocks, the structure is designed to support photovoltaic and hybrid solar-plus-storage projects, specifically targeting daytime and evening peak coverage requirements.

tech.headline: SNEC 2026 shows solar shifting toward system integration and AI
tech.summary: SNEC 2026 highlighted a significant industry shift from module-centric growth toward system integration. Storage and AI are becoming increasingly central to project design and value creation. At the technology level, back-contact technology gained momentum as a premium platform, while emerging applications and scenario-specific modules signaled a trend toward deeper market segmentation within the solar sector.

tech.headline: Longi develops 27.27% efficient heterojunction back-contact solar cell
tech.summary: Longi and Yangzhou University researchers developed a laser-based manufacturing strategy for HJT back-contact solar cells. This method mitigates shock-wave-induced damage, improving passivation stability and device performance. The approach resulted in a certified 27.27% efficient solar cell, providing an industrially viable pathway toward the fabrication of ultra-high-efficiency photovoltaic technology through improved laser processing techniques.

learn.title: Solar Inverters: String, Micro, and Hybrid
learn.intro: Solar panels produce Direct Current (DC), but our homes and grids run on Alternating Current (AC). Inverters are the critical 'translators' of the system. Choosing the right inverter determines how much energy you actually harvest, especially when dealing with shade or battery storage, making them the brain of any photovoltaic installation.
learn.c1_text: The traditional choice. Like a single power strip for a group of lamps, one inverter manages a whole 'string' of panels. If one panel is shaded, the entire string's performance drops to that lowest level, limiting efficiency in complex roof layouts.
learn.c2_text: These are small inverters installed on every single panel. Think of it as giving every panel its own personal manager. This eliminates the 'bottleneck' effect of string inverters, allowing each panel to produce maximum power regardless of what its neighbor is doing.
learn.c3_text: These combine a standard inverter with a battery manager. In India, as residential storage becomes popular, hybrid systems allow homeowners to store excess day-time energy for night use, reducing dependence on unstable local grid connections during peak hours.
learn.stat2: Micro-inverters can increase energy yield by 5-15%

learn.title: Power Purchase Agreements (PPAs) Explained
learn.intro: Building massive wind or solar farms requires huge upfront capital. PPAs solve this by guaranteeing a buyer for the electricity before the project is even built. This financial certainty allows developers to secure loans and drive down the cost of clean energy for everyone.
learn.c1_text: A PPA is a long-term contract between a power producer and a buyer. It is like a pre-order for electricity for 10 to 25 years. The buyer agrees to pay a fixed price per kWh, protecting both parties from volatile market price swings.
learn.c2_text: Companies like Google or Amazon use Corporate PPAs to meet sustainability goals. By committing to buy green power, they provide the 'bankability' needed for developers to build new renewable plants rather than just buying credits from old ones.
learn.c3_text: In India, SECI (Solar Energy Corporation of India) often acts as an intermediary, aggregating demand to ensure developers get guaranteed payments, which has helped India achieve some of the lowest solar tariffs globally.
learn.stat2: Global corporate RE procurement: Multi-GW annual growth
learn.stat3: India: SECI manages some of world's largest solar tenders
learn.stat4: Fixed tariffs reduce price volatility by ~100%

learn.title: Concentrated Solar Power (CSP)
learn.intro: Unlike PV panels that convert light directly to electricity, CSP uses mirrors to concentrate sunlight into heat. This thermal energy can be stored, meaning CSP can generate electricity even after the sun goes down, solving the intermittency problem that plagues standard solar power.
learn.c1_text: CSP uses heliostats—thousands of tracking mirrors—to focus sunlight onto a single point. It is like using a magnifying glass to start a fire, creating intense heat that boils water or melts salt to drive a turbine.
learn.c2_text: Molten salt is often used to store this heat. Because salt holds heat exceptionally well, CSP plants can continue generating power for 6-12 hours after sunset, providing 'baseload' power that PV panels cannot provide without batteries.
learn.c3_text: India has explored CSP in high-irradiation zones like Rajasthan. While more expensive than PV, CSP is vital for India's goal of 500 GW non-fossil capacity by 2030 due to its dispatchable nature.
learn.stat3: India Target: 500 GW non-fossil fuel capacity (2030)

learn.title: Understanding Levelised Cost of Energy (LCOE)
learn.intro: How do we know if wind is cheaper than coal? We can't just look at the construction cost. LCOE provides a 'single number' that accounts for every cent spent over the project's entire life, allowing a fair apples-to-apples comparison between different energy sources.
learn.c1_text: LCOE adds up all costs: construction, operation, maintenance, and fuel, then divides it by the total energy produced over 20-30 years. It is like calculating the 'per-mile cost' of a car, including gas, insurance, and the purchase price.
learn.c2_text: Renewables have high CAPEX (upfront cost) but near-zero OPEX (fuel is free). Fossil fuels have lower CAPEX but high, volatile OPEX. LCOE reveals that renewables are now often cheaper because the 'fuel' cost is zero.
learn.c3_text: India has seen a dramatic drop in solar LCOE over the last decade, driven by competitive bidding and scale, making solar the cheapest source of new electricity in the country.
learn.stat3: Solar LCOE in India: among world's lowest

learn.title: Renewable Purchase Obligations (RPO) in India
learn.intro: To ensure the energy transition happens quickly, the government doesn't just encourage renewables—it mandates them. RPOs force electricity distributors (DISCOMs) to buy a minimum percentage of their power from renewable sources, creating a guaranteed market for green energy.
learn.c1_text: RPOs are regulatory requirements. If a DISCOM fails to meet its quota, it must pay a penalty or buy 'Renewable Energy Certificates' (RECs). It is like a quota system that forces a diet of green energy on the grid.
learn.c2_text: DISCOMs often struggle with financial health. Meeting RPOs can be difficult when they are already in debt, leading to a gap between the mandated targets and the actual amount of green energy purchased.
learn.c3_text: The Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (MNRE) sets these targets to drive investment. This policy has pushed India to rapidly scale its wind and solar capacity to meet the increasing percentage requirements.
learn.stat3: RECs act as a market-based compliance tool
learn.stat4: India: 4th largest installed RE capacity globally

learn.title: Second Life EV Batteries for Grid Storage
learn.intro: EV batteries are replaced when they can no longer power a car, but they aren't 'dead.' A battery at 70% capacity is useless for a Tesla but perfect for storing solar power for a building, creating a circular economy that reduces waste.
learn.c1_text: An EV battery is 'retired' when its capacity drops below ~80%. However, for grid storage, 70% capacity is still massive. It is like using an old laptop battery to power a small LED light; it's not enough for the laptop, but plenty for the light.
learn.c2_text: Second-life batteries provide 'peak shaving.' They store energy when demand is low and release it during peak hours, preventing grid crashes and reducing the need for expensive gas-fired 'peaker' plants.
learn.c3_text: As India's EV adoption grows, the volume of retired batteries will surge. Implementing second-life policies can lower the cost of grid storage for Indian villages, making decentralized micro-grids more affordable.
learn.stat2: Potential cost reduction: 30-50% vs new batteries
learn.stat3: Battery recycling efficiency: up to 95% of metals
learn.stat4: EV sales growth in India: ~150% CAGR (recent years)
